cluster_name: Ukraine's largest Moscow drone attack strikes Kapotnya oil refinery second time
tldr: Headlines across all 14 country buckets converge on a single visual — Ukraine's record drone barrage setting Moscow's Kapotnya oil refinery ablaze — while a secondary tier in Baltic, German, and Indian titles converts that spectacle into an economic frame (Russia's deepening fuel crisis). Two sub-narratives emerge sharply at headline level that body text only develops later: a 'black rain' environmental-hazard frame in UK and Indian titles, and an explicit political-threat exchange (Zelenskyy's 'Moscow will burn too,' Putin's 'massive retaliation') that the homepage layer presents more starkly than the articles themselves.

evidence.quote: Ukraine hits Moscow refinery in major drone attack
Ukraine sets Moscow ablaze in biggest drone attack in years
Is Russia heading for a fuel crisis?
Experts: Russia's fuel crisis keeps getting worse
Massive Drone Barrage Deepens Russia's Fuel Crisis
Moscow residents complain of black rain
Putin warns of 'massive' retaliation for refinery attack
says strikes on Ukraine will continue
claims Moscow air defences worked well – videos
Russia strikes leave historic Kyiv cathedral in flames

single_outlet_findings.finding: Fox News is the only corpus outlet to use visceral emotional language in the headline itself — ''Pure hell' in Moscow' — while all other US, UK, and European titles use neutral factual register ('major drone attack', 'hits refinery'). The sensational register is surface-visible at RSS/homepage level before any click.

Globe and Mail leads with a Ukrainian drone attack on St. Petersburg rather than Moscow — the only headline in the corpus to frame the story as geographic escalation spreading beyond the capital, rather than a Moscow-refinery strike.

DW English is the only outlet whose headline foregrounds Russia's counter-strike on Ukraine — specifically the burning of the Kyiv Pechersk Lavra UNESCO monastery — inverting the dominant Moscow-attack framing visible in every other corpus title.

Both Ukrinform English titles cover Russian artillery and drone attacks on Ukrainian regions (Dnipropetrovsk, Mykolaiv) rather than Ukraine's own Moscow strike — the only Ukraine-bucket outlet whose homepage framing centres Ukrainian civilian victimisation rather than the offensive campaign.

bottom_line: At the headline layer, Ukraine's record drone barrage on Moscow's Kapotnya refinery achieves near-total global convergence on a single visual frame — refinery ablaze, airports shut, 'biggest attack in years' — while Baltic and German outlets use secondary titles to surface Russia's fuel crisis that most homepage readers will never click through to find.
